followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: I keep the overall verdict as promising but lower the score slightly, mainly because the other reviews reinforce that matrix-element uncertainty, background realism, and consistent comparison to astrophysical bounds are central to the leading-constraint claim. I do not adopt the fatal 'missing full text' objection because my prior cycle had section- and equation-level content to review, even though the supplied bundle excerpt is incomplete.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: The claim that technical verification is impossible or that the full text is fatally missing is not a substantive objection to the paper itself in this follow-up setting, because the prior review included equation-level access and specific local checks.

The suggestion that scalar attenuation through shielding may be important near the detection threshold is weakly grounded; for the feeble couplings relevant to reach plots, attenuation of the new particle is likely negligible compared with source-rate and detector-background uncertainties unless the paper studies much larger couplings.

The statement that SNO scalar recasting must account for polarization differences is not well posed for a CP-even scalar deuteron-dissociation process; the real issue is the correct scalar production flux, matrix element, detector response, and coupling normalization.
The Big Bang Theory fan-blog references should not affect the scientific verdict.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: Concerns about nuclear matrix-element uncertainties for lithium-related production are valid in spirit and align with my prior objection about NDA scalar production estimates.
Concerns about near-reactor neutron and gamma backgrounds are valid and should be elevated because the detection concept depends on a radiation environment not demonstrated in the paper excerpt.
The request to check astrophysical and stellar-cooling exclusions is valid for the 'leading constraints' claim, although the other reviews could not assess the actual figures.
The Big Bang Theory citations are real but scientifically harmless; at most they are a style oddity.

main_claims.claim: Fusion reactors can produce fluxes of exotic spin-0 particles through neutron capture and neutron scattering in breeding-blanket or structural materials.
main_claims.notes: The mechanism is physically motivated and analogous to known nuclear axion production settings. The support is limited by simplified reactor geometry and flux modeling rather than by the basic idea.
main_claims.paper_location: Abstract; Sections 1 and 3 as cited in prior cycle, especially Eqs. (3.1)-(3.4) and Section 3.3

main_claims.claim: Year-long searches at current or future fusion reactors can set leading constraints on scalar- and pseudoscalar-nucleon couplings.
main_claims.notes: This remains plausible for benchmark assumptions, but it is the claim most exposed to normalization, background, and existing-bound comparison uncertainties.
main_claims.paper_location: Abstract; Section 5; Figures 7 and 8 as cited in prior cycle

main_claims.claim: Scalar production in neutron absorption and scattering can be estimated by scaling known electromagnetic or neutron-scattering cross sections using NDA.
main_claims.notes: The authors appear transparent that these estimates are rough. Other reviewers' matrix-element concerns reinforce, but do not independently sharpen, this objection.
main_claims.paper_location: Section 3.1, Eqs. (3.8)-(3.9); Appendix D as cited in prior cycle

main_claims.claim: A SNO-like heavy-water detector near a fusion reactor can detect the new flux through deuteron dissociation.
main_claims.notes: The detection channel is well motivated, but the written event-rate formulas and detector-mass wording contain local issues, and the near-reactor background model remains underdeveloped.
main_claims.paper_location: Section 4, Eqs. (4.1)-(4.7) as cited in prior cycle

main_claims.claim: The SNO axion bound can be recast for a CP-even scalar.
main_claims.notes: The recast is a reasonable phenomenological move, but the bundle and prior excerpts do not provide enough intermediate numerical detail for me to audit the final exclusion region.
main_claims.paper_location: Section 5 discussion around Figure 8 as cited in prior cycle

missing_assumptions.assumption: An explicit first-wall area or neutron wall-loading normalization is needed to convert reactor power into the flux used in production rates.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Projected rates are proportional to neutron flux; an area or wall-loading normalization error would shift all sensitivity curves.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The effective target number N_X and attenuation factor epsilon_atten = 0.1 should be justified for the same neutron spectrum and geometry used in the cross-section convolution.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The simplified accessible-target model replaces a transport calculation, so consistency among attenuation, neutron spectrum, material composition, and geometry controls the flux normalization.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Suppression of neutron couplings while retaining proton couplings should be tied to viable UV flavor structures or explicitly labeled as a benchmark-only assumption.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Section 2 benchmarks, Eqs. (2.12)-(2.13); Section 3 and Section 4 repeatedly set g_phi_n = 0
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The projected reach and evasion of neutron-scattering constraints rely on this coupling pattern.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Near-reactor neutron and gamma backgrounds after shielding need a quantitative detector and facility model.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: A SNO-like detector 10 m from a fusion reactor is not in a SNO-like radiation environment; backgrounds may dominate the claimed S/sqrt(B) reach.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Dedicated nuclear matrix elements, or at least uncertainty bands over plausible nuclear-production rates, are needed for scalar exclusion projections.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The scalar reach can move substantially if NDA scaling over- or underestimates the true emission rates.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The comparison against astrophysical, beam-dump, fission-reactor, and SNO-derived bounds must be made in exactly the same coupling conventions and benchmark hierarchy.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Other reviewers correctly noted that 'leading constraints' is only meaningful if all external exclusions are consistently translated into the paper's scalar and pseudoscalar coupling normalizations.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the published numerical implementation use the correct 1/L^2 detector-distance scaling despite Eqs. (4.6)-(4.7) apparently showing (L/10 m)^2?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The paper text exposes the formula, but not the plotting code or notebooks behind Figures 7 and 8.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the neutron flux normalization in Eq. (3.10) implemented with a first-wall area, neutron wall loading, or another geometry factor?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The excerpted text and prior notes identify a dimensional issue but do not reveal enough implementation detail to determine whether only the displayed equation is wrong.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Are the scalar NDA production estimates within an order of magnitude of realistic nuclear calculations for the relevant Li and Fe transitions?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This requires domain-specific nuclear-structure calculations or detailed comparison with dedicated matrix elements beyond the local review text.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Can a SNO-like detector near ITER, DEMO, or ARC plausibly reduce reactor neutron and gamma backgrounds below the background model used in the sensitivity estimates?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This depends on facility layout, shielding design, detector siting, and radiation-transport simulations not provided here.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Are all external bounds in Figures 7 and 8 translated into the same scalar and pseudoscalar nucleon-coupling conventions as the paper's benchmarks?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This requires auditing the recast choices, coupling normalizations, and possibly the authors' plotting code, not just the narrative text.

novelty_assessment: I continue to view the source concept as genuinely novel and timely: applying fusion-reactor breeding blankets, rather than solar or fission-reactor environments, to light spin-0 particle searches is a meaningful phenomenological extension. The novelty is strongest at the level of source proposal and benchmark reach estimates. It is not a new theoretical framework, and the detector/recast ingredients are adapted from established axion, ALP, reactor, and SNO methods.
score_rationale: The paper remains a creative and potentially useful phenomenology proposal, and the core production/detection idea is plausible. I lower the score slightly from cycle 1 because the other reviews reinforce that the quantitative 'leading constraints' claim rests on several fragile ingredients: rough scalar nuclear production, simplified reactor flux/geometry modeling, near-reactor backgrounds, and consistency of external-bound recasts. I reject the fatal missing-text objection as a review-process limitation rather than a paper flaw. Overall: promising concept, but not yet technically secure enough for a higher score.
summary: The paper proposes fusion-reactor breeding blankets as intense sources of light scalar or pseudoscalar particles produced by neutron capture or scattering, with detection via a nearby SNO-like heavy-water setup and a recast SNO scalar bound. After reading the other reviews, I keep my prior view: this is a genuinely interesting phenomenological proposal, but the quantitative reach is not yet something I would treat as certified. The most substantive concerns remain normalization, detector-distance scaling, simplified reactor geometry/background assumptions, and rough scalar nuclear-production estimates.

technical_objections.evidence: Eq. (3.11) defines the spin-zero flux at the detector with a 1/(4 pi L^2) factor, so the number of events for a fixed detector should decrease as 1/L^2. The displayed Eqs. (4.6)-(4.7), according to my prior reading, instead contain factors proportional to (L/10 m)^2.
technical_objections.objection: The event-rate estimates scale with detector distance in the wrong direction as written.

technical_objections.evidence: The expression Phi_n^total approximately P/14.1 MeV has units of neutrons per second, not neutrons cm^-2 s^-1. A surface-area normalization is needed before using it as a flux in later cross-section estimates.
technical_objections.objection: The neutron flux normalization in Eq. (3.10) is dimensionally incomplete unless an implicit first-wall area or wall-loading conversion is being assumed.

technical_objections.evidence: The prior review found that scalar absorption and scattering estimates are explicitly presented as rough guidance without dedicated nuclear matrix-element calculations. Other reviewers' concerns about Li nuclear matrix-element uncertainties are valid in spirit, though less grounded from their limited text access.
technical_objections.objection: The scalar production reach is driven by rough NDA production estimates not independently validated in the paper.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section 3.1, especially Eqs. (3.8)-(3.9), and Section 6 caveats

technical_objections.evidence: The analysis uses a SNO-like detector concept close to a fusion facility. Other reviewers correctly emphasize that fusion-reactor neutron and gamma backgrounds are facility-specific and cannot be assumed comparable to SNO without transport/shielding studies.
technical_objections.objection: The feasibility estimate relies on optimistic or insufficiently specified near-reactor background control.

technical_objections.evidence: N_D around 6e31 is appropriate for roughly 1000 tonnes of D2O, while one metric ton contains about 6e28 deuterium atoms. This is likely a wording error because the setup is SNO-like, but it should be corrected.
technical_objections.objection: The deuterium target count is described as corresponding to one metric ton, but numerically corresponds to about 1000 tonnes of heavy water.

technical_objections.evidence: A neutron-per-energy estimate gives roughly one D-T neutron per 17.6 MeV versus order 2-3 fission neutrons per about 200 MeV, only a factor of a few before geometry, moderation, and wall-loading details. The claim may be true for local flux in specific blanket geometries, but the basis should be stated.
technical_objections.objection: The comparison claim that fusion reactors have neutron flux about two orders of magnitude higher than fission reactors of comparable power is insufficiently justified as stated.
technical_objections.paper_location: Introduction, first page of the published PDF as cited in prior cycle

technical_objections.evidence: My prior reading found Appendix A introducing a neutron-density operator while the main benchmark sets g_phi_n = 0 and uses proton coupling in Eq. (4.3). This is probably notation slippage, but it obscures the exact one-body operator used.
technical_objections.objection: The scalar deuterium-dissociation appendix uses notation that conflicts with the proton-only benchmark used in the main text.
technical_objections.paper_location: Appendix A, Eqs. (A.1)-(A.9), compared with Section 2 benchmarks and Eq. (4.3)

technical_objections.evidence: The text reportedly refers to Eq. (3.9) for an ALP non-resonant neutron-absorption estimate, while Eq. (3.9) is the scalar neutron-scattering estimate; the Figure 7 caption points to Eq. (3.7), which appears intended.
technical_objections.objection: There is an incorrect equation reference in the projected-sensitivity discussion for ALP non-resonant capture.
